title: The High Cost of People Pleasing
reading:
Three stories. Three people. One pattern.

Pilate knew Jesus was innocent. Matthew 27:24 says he saw that he could not prevail — but when the crowd demanded crucifixion, he washed his hands and handed Jesus over. The Son of God was delivered to death in part because a Roman governor refused to be unpopular.

Peter had eaten with Gentile believers freely — until certain men arrived from Jerusalem. Galatians 2:11-12 records what happened next: he withdrew and separated himself, fearing those of the circumcision. Paul confronted him publicly because the issue was serious. Peter's people pleasing wasn't just social discomfort — it was a betrayal of the gospel.

The parents of the man born blind knew what had happened. Their son could see. But John 9:20-22 records that they deflected every question about Jesus because they feared what it might cost them. They chose social safety over truth.

The stakes of people pleasing are rarely as dramatic as these three stories. But the principle is the same: when approval from people becomes more important than faithfulness to God, we compromise. And often, the person who suffers is not just us.
reflection: Is there a place in your life where fear of what others think is keeping you from full obedience? What would it cost you to change that?
abide: Jesus, forgive me for the times I have prioritized being liked over being faithful. Give me a courage that comes from knowing You — not from needing people's approval.
